segments.s244-g9: بپرسید here is a past-tense narrative verb 'asked' (third person) used in the usual SOV/verb-final narrative style; the clause begins with the source/agent ز ترکان 'from the Turks' (literally 'asked of the Turks'). کین دار چیست is an interrogative nominal clause 'what is this dar' with چیست (چی + ست) as the copula 'is' attached after the questioned noun.
segments.s244-g10: This is a short interrogative clause whose head is a locative/positional phrase: در شاه را 'at the king's court/door' (شاه + را for definiteness/emphasis). از در دار is a genitive/partitive phrase 'from the door/of the dar' and کیست is the fused interrogative 'who is (it)'. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: 'درِ شاه را ازِ درِ دار کیست' = 'who is (he) at the king's court from the Dar?'
segments.s244-g11: بدو 'to him' is an indirect-object/prepositional phrase placed before the verb; گفت here functions as the past-tense verb 'said' (narrative past). The speaker's name follows the verb (گرتیوز as subject after گفت) and the quoted/predicative clause این بیژنست 'this is Bijan' is a simple nominal sentence with the copula attached to the proper name.
segments.s244-g12: This is a short explanatory/question clause: از ایران 'from Iran' indicates origin, followed by the interrogative/exclamatory کجا which here functions rhetorically as 'how/why' (not a pure 'where'), and شاه را دشمنست is a nominal predicate 'he is the king's enemy' with را marking 'the king' as a definite/affected noun. The structure asks how someone from Iran could be the king's enemy.
segments.s244-g13: بزد اسب is a compact poetic clause meaning 'he struck (the) horse' (i.e., urged it to move); the verb بزد (past) precedes the object (اسب) as a terse action. و آمد بر + name uses آمد (came) plus the preposition بر meaning 'to/onto/toward' followed by the target بیژنا (the proper name in oblique form) to mean 'and came up to Bijan.'
segments.s244-g14: This is a sequence of descriptive phrases about the seen person: جگر خسته is an adjectival/nominal phrase 'heart weary' (i.e. emotionally/exhaustively tired), and دیدش is the verb 'he saw him' with the object clitic -ش. برهنه تنا places the adjective برهنه 'naked/exposed' before the noun تنا 'body' (poetic order), describing his physical condition. The order emphasizes state (jگر خسته) then the act of seeing then physical description.
segments.s244-g15: دو دست is a quantified noun phrase 'two hands.' از پس پشت بسته is a prepositional/adverbial cluster 'bound from behind the back' (از پسِ پشت = behind the back) with بسته 'tied/bound' as the predicate describing the hands; چو سنگ is a simile 'like a stone' modifying بسته. Poetic compounding and inversion are used to pack the image into a short phrase.
segments.s244-g16: This is a pair of linked nominal/predicative phrases: دهن خشک 'mouth dry' (i.e. parched mouth) functions as a brief predicate, coordinated by و with the following verbless/perfective clause رفته ز رخساره رنگ 'the color has gone from the face.' Here رفته is the perfective 'has gone' and ز introduces the source 'from his face' (رخساره), with رنگ as the affected subject placed after the verb in poetic order.
segments.s244-g169: خروشان is an adverbial/adjective used here to qualify the coming (literally “roaring”); بیامد is a past-tense 3rd-person singular verb 'came'; بنزدیک is a prepositional/adverbial compound meaning 'near/close at hand' placed before the noun; چاه is the definite object 'well' (here location). The phrase order is subject/description + verb + location.
segments.s244-g170: یکی is an indefinite pronoun 'someone' (here introducing an unspecified actor). دست is the noun 'hand' and را is the accusative/definite-object marker attached to it. اندرو is a fused locative 'into it/within it' (اندَر + او), and کرد راه is a light-verbal construction: کرد (past 'made/did') plus راه 'way/path' → 'made a way' or 'cleared a passage.' Word order: actor (یکی) + direct object (دست را) + locative (اندرو) + verb phrase (کرد راه).
segments.s244-g171: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' از marks origin 'from/out of,' کوه 'mountain,' خورشید 'the sun.' سر is 'head' used figuratively with برزدی (an archaic/poetic verb form) meaning 'lifted/raised its head' i.e. 'rose.' The verb is archaic/poetic in form, so the clause reads 'when the sun rose from the mountain.'
segments.s244-g172: منیژه is the proper name 'Manijeh' as subject. ز is the poetic preposition 'from,' هر در literally 'every door/gate' (هر = 'each/every,' در = 'door'); همی is the habitual/continuous particle meaning 'continually' or an aspectual marker; نان is 'bread.' چدی here is lexically unclear in the provided senses (none fit cleanly) so it likely functions as a verb/verb-form meaning something like 'gathered/served/baked,' but that sense is not among the supplied senses.
segments.s244-g173: همی is the habitual/continuous particle 'kept/was doing.' گرد کردی is a compound verbal phrase: گرد (around) + کردی (2nd-person past 'you did') → 'you went about/you circled.' بروز is a temporal/locative 'on the day' or 'during the day,' and دراز is the adjective 'long' modifying بروز, together 'on that long day.' Word order places the adverbial/time phrase after the verb phrase in this poetic style.
segments.s244-g174: بسوراخ is a prepositional/locative compound 'into/from the hole' (بـ + سوراخ) indicating origin/exit from a hole; چاه specifies the hole as 'the well'; آوریدی is the 2nd-person past 'you brought' (perfective); فراز is an adverb/noun 'to the top' or 'up on high.' The sequence expresses motion out of the well (بسوراخ چاه) and the bringing up (آوریدی) to the top (فراز).
segments.s244-g175: ببیژن (بِ+بیژن) is a prepositional phrase 'to/with Bijan' indicating recipient; سپردی is the 2nd-person past 'you handed over/entrusted.' و is the coordinating conjunction 'and.' بگریستی is 2nd-person past 'you wept.' Together this clause lists two successive past actions performed by the same subject (you).
segments.s244-g176: بران is a demonstrative/prepositional 'on/over/with that' referring back to the previous matter; شوربختی is the noun 'misfortune/misery'; همی again marks continuing state ('still/in continuance'); زیستی is 2nd-person past 'you lived.' The phrase reads 'you lived in/under that misfortune' with همی emphasizing the ongoing nature of that life.
segments.s244-g153: This phrase groups an instrument/quality and the agent before the verb: بپولاد is an adverbial/qualifying element meaning ‘with steel’ or ‘steel‑clad’, خایسک is an obscure/dialectal adjective or epithet modifying the agent, and آهنگران is the plural subject 'the blacksmiths'. Poetic word order places the modifier(s) before the head noun.
segments.s244-g154: فروبرد is perfect (past) 3rd person plural 'they thrust/drove in'. Its object is مسمارهای گران: مسمارهای is the plural (mashdar + plural suffix -ها + ezāfe-like construct) 'nails/pegs' and گران is a post‑nominal adjective 'heavy/weighty' (Persian normally places adjectives after nouns). Together: 'they drove in (the) heavy nails.'
segments.s244-g155: نگونش is نگون (an adjective 'ill‑fated, doomed') with the third‑person object/possessive clitic -ش attached, functioning as 'him/the doomed one'. بچاه here is the locative expression 'به چاه/into the well' (written together), اندر is an archaic preposition 'in/within' used poetically, and انداختند is past 3rd‑pl. 'they threw/cast'. The sequence yields 'they cast him into the well.'
segments.s244-g156: سر چاه را is a noun phrase 'the top/mouth of the well' with the definite/object marker را. بند here means a closure/fastening (a noun) and بر is the preposition 'on/upon'; ساختند is past 3rd‑pl. 'they made/built'. Together: 'they made/placed a closure upon the well's mouth.' Note the object marker را marks سر چاه as the definite thing being acted on.
segments.s244-g157: وز آنجا is a compact, archaic/poetic temporal‑locative expression equivalent to 'and/from there/then'; وز here is a poetic particle (often written و از). بایوان is an archaic/uncertain word for a place (contextually 'portico/porch' or similar locative), and آن دخترش is the demonstrative plus possessed noun 'that daughter of his' placed before the verb for emphasis. The whole segment functions as an adverbial phrase specifying place and the direct object to be brought.
segments.s244-g158: بیاورد is past 3rd‑singular 'he brought' (or 'he caused to be brought'); گرسیوز is the proper name 'Garsivaz' (the subject/agent here). آن is the demonstrative 'that/the' and لشکرش is 'his army/host' (لشکر + possessive -ش). The clause reads 'Garsivaz brought that army/his host' or, with the previous segment, 'Garsivaz brought that daughter into the portico (with his army).' Possessive suffix on لشکر marks ownership.
segments.s244-g159: همه introduces a total object 'all' and lists گنج و گوهر 'treasure and jewels' joined by و. بتاراج is a verbal/adverbial participle meaning 'as plunder/looted' placed before the verb for emphasis, and داد is the past 3rd‑singular 'he gave'. The clause means 'he gave all the treasure and jewels as plunder.' Note داد here is the lexical verb 'gave' (not the abstract noun 'justice').
segments.s244-g160: This compact, somewhat archaic clause parses as follows: ازین = از + این 'from/of this', بدره is a poetic epithet 'full‑moon' used metaphorically for a beautiful woman, بستد is past 'he bound/fastened' or 'he fixed', and بدان is the prepositional phrase بِ + آن 'to that/her' functioning as the indirect object; تاج is 'crown' and داد is again 'he gave'. In effect: 'From this moon‑like beauty he bound/gave that crown to her.' Several items are archaic or written in contracted form (e.g. بد اِن → بدان).
segments.s244-g105: چنینست is a fixed copular phrase meaning “thus it is.” کین here appears before شاه and functions as a determiner/marker (archaic/useful as “this/that”), so the whole clause reads literally “thus is this king says”; گوید is the 3rd-person present of گفتن (‘says’). همی is the old Persian progressive/continuative particle attached after the verb meaning that the saying is habitual or ongoing (“keeps saying” / “regularly says”).
segments.s244-g106: جز از ... نجوید همی is a negative-exceptive clause: جز از = “except for / except from.” نیک‌نامی is the object (a good name, renown). نجوید is the 3rd-person present subjunctive/indicative of جستن/جوییـدن here meaning “seeks” or “desires,” and the sentence-final همی again marks habitual/continuous aspect: “he seeks (continually) nothing except renown.” Word order is Subject–(negation)–Object–Verb typical of classical Persian.
segments.s244-g107: ولیکن is the adversative conjunction “but/yet.” بدین is a compound of the prefix بـ (ب = “in/to/with”) + دین here functioning as the demonstrative/locution ‘this’ in classical style (so بدین = “in this / to this”). رای means “opinion / counsel,” and هشیارِ من is a vocative or appositive “my alert/prudent one” (هشیار modified by the possessive من after it, a common poetic order).
segments.s244-g108: یکی بنگرد is an indefinite jussive/imperative: یکی = “someone / let one,” and بنگرد is the present-subjunctive/imperative of نگریستن (‘look/consider’) meaning “let (one) look / consider.” ژرف qualifies the manner (“deeply, thoroughly”) and سالار من is a vocative ‘my commander/chief’ (noun followed by the possessor من). The clause asks that someone (or the addressee) consider this counsel deeply.
segments.s244-g109: ببندد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive or jussive form of بستن/بندیدن here used imperatively/performative (“bind”). مر = me (dative/beneficiary: “to/for me”), او را is the accusative object (“him”), and then ببند (imperative) repeats the command (“bind!”). گران is an adverbial/qualifying adjective here meaning “firmly / heavily / tightly” modifying the verb ‘bind.’ The clause is a direct command to bind the man and deliver him into the speaker’s custody.
segments.s244-g110: کجا دار is an interrogative/indeterminate phrase meaning “where (is) the دار” — دار in epic/older Persian often denotes the place of execution (gallows) or a site for punishment. و is the coordinating conjunction “and,” کشتن is the infinitive/noun ‘killing’ or verb ‘to kill,’ and گزیند is the 3rd-person present of گزیدن (‘choose’): “choose (it).” بران = ‘there/on that’ (indicating place). Together: order to choose a place of execution and execute him there.
segments.s244-g111: هر آنکو is a relative/indefinite phrase “whoever / any one who.” بزندان is the prepositional/locative compound (بـ + زندان) meaning “in prison/into prison.” تو is the 2nd-person subject or agent “you,” بسته (participle/adjective) = “bound/kept shut,” and ماند is the 3rd-person present of ماندن ‘remain’ (here: “remains confined”). The clause states a general consequence for anyone you keep imprisoned.
segments.s244-g112: ز (poetic form of از) = “from” or “out of.” دیوانها is the plural of دیوان and here means the official registers/record books (not a poetry-collection). نام او = “his name,” کس in this negative context means “no one,” and نخواند is the future/irrealis 3rd-person (or present with negative) of خواندن used here in the sense “to read/recite or to call (a name)” — together: “no one will read / will call his name from the rolls.”
segments.s244-g145: 'بهارش' is a possessive noun (بهار + ـش) meaning "his spring/youth." In this line the noun is followed by the adverbial particle 'توی' used predicatively (poetic usage) to mean "is within/inside," so the phrase reads roughly "his spring is within." The same pattern repeats for 'غمگسارش توی' where 'غمگسار' is a substantivized adjective/noun "comforter/one who shares sorrow" with the same 'توی' predicate.
segments.s244-g146: 'درین' is the composite deictic 'در' + old/poetic 'ین' (= این), i.e. "in this." 'تنگ زندان' is an adjective + noun pair "narrow prison/dungeon." 'زوارش' is 'زوار' + possessive suffix 'ش' and here means the emaciated/wrinkled frame of the person (folds/wrinkles = emaciation); again 'توی' is used predicatively "lies/is within." The order is poetic and elliptical: "in this narrow prison his emaciation is within."
segments.s244-g147: The verb 'خرامید' (3rd‑singular past) precedes the proper name as a poetically inverted word order: "strode/pranced Garsivaz." 'از پیش اوی' means "in front of/before him," where 'اوی' is the third‑person pronoun "him/his."
segments.s244-g148: 'بکردند' is a past plural verb "they did/they caused." The object is 'کام' (wish/desire), followed by 'بداندیش اوی' — 'بداندیش' (malicious/ill‑minded) here functions substantivally ("the ill‑wisher/evil‑minded one") and 'اوی' is the possessive/3rd‑person marker "his." So the clause means "they did (fulfilled) the wish of his malicious one/foe."
segments.s244-g149: 'کشان' is a verbal participle meaning "dragging" and introduces the manner of action. It attaches to the following names 'بیژن گیو' (Bijan, Giv). 'از پیش دار' is a locative phrase "from/in front of the gallows" (دار here = "gallows"). The order is poetic: participle + objects/names + adverbial phrase.
segments.s244-g150: 'ببردند' is past plural "they took/carried." 'بسته' is a participle/adjective "bound/tied" modifying the (implied) person. 'بران' is a deictic adverb "to/on that (place)," and 'چاهسار' is a place name/term "well‑field/area of wells." Together: "they took him, bound, to that well‑field."
segments.s244-g151: The correlative 'ز ... تا ...' marks a range: 'ز سر تا به پایش' = "from head to his foot." 'به آهن ببست' is the verb phrase where 'به' marks the instrument/material "with/by iron" and 'ببست' (3rd‑singular past) means "(he/they) bound/tied." The possessive suffix 'ش' on 'پایش' = "his foot." The poetic subject (they/men) is understood from context.
segments.s244-g152: This is a simple enumerative phrase listing body parts preceded by the preposition 'بر' (= "on/upon"): 'بر و بازوی و گردن و پای و دست' = "on the chest/torso and the arm(s) and the neck and the foot/feet and the hand(s)." The repeated 'و' links the list; the whole phrase specifies the places bound by the iron.
segments.s244-g129: فگندست is an archaic/poetic perfective verb 'threw' (3rd‑person). در introduces the location 'into/in'. بیشه چین ستان is a noun phrase 'the grove (بیشه) of Chin(چین)‑stan(ستان)' (چین+ستان = Chinestān); word order is verb + در + place phrase, typical of Persian narrative.
segments.s244-g130: بیاور is an imperative 'bring!'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. بیژن is the personal name Bijan (object/source). ب + دان here combine as the deictic/adverbial 'بدان' meaning 'to/with that' (literally بِ + آن) — a small fused prepositional phrase directing the following action toward 'that'. کین ستان is a genitive-like compound 'land/place of vengeance' (کین = vengeance, ستان = land/place).
segments.s244-g131: بپیلان is an obscure/poetic form (here functioning as an instruction or verb phrase involving elephants — likely 'by/with the elephants' or 'cause the elephants to ...'). گردون کش is a compound where گردون 'the sky/heaven' combines with کش 'puller' (agentive/verb stem) to mean 'sky‑pulling' or 'heaven‑dragging'; آن سنگ را is the definite object 'that stone' marked by را.
segments.s244-g132: که is the relative pronoun 'which/that' introducing a relative clause. پوشد is 3rd‑person present/subjunctive from پوشیدن meaning 'cover/overlie' (here 'covers'). سر چاه ارژنگ را is the noun phrase 'the head/top of Arzhang's well' with را marking the whole NP as definite/object of پوشد.
segments.s244-g133: بیاور is an imperative 'bring!'. سر چاه او is a possessed NP 'the head/top of his well' (سر = top/head, followed by the possessed noun چاه and the pronoun او). را marks this NP as definite/object. بپوش is an imperative/subjunctive 'cover (it)' (بپوش = be + پوش or an imperative form meaning 'put on/cover').
segments.s244-g134: بدان here is the fused prepositional/adverbial 'with that / thereby' (بِ + آن) — pointing to the stone already mentioned. تا introduces a purposive clause 'so that'. بزاری is used as 'the wretched one' (noun/adjective 'wretched'); برآیدش is a 3rd‑person verb 'may arise/return' with the enclitic pronoun ش attached (here 'his [sense] will return'); هوش is 'awareness/sense' — so the clause means 'so that the wretched one may regain his senses.'
segments.s244-g135: وز آنجا is a connective 'and from there' (وز = and/from, آنجا = there). بایوان is an archaic/uncertain verbal/formal element (here functioning to command or indicate fetching — conjecturally 'bring' or 'let be brought'); آن بی‌هنر is the demonstrative + adjective 'that unskilled (woman)' modifying the following proper name.
segments.s244-g136: منیژه is the personal name 'Manijeh'. کزو = (ک+زو) a fused relativizer/compound meaning 'from whom/from which' (ک = relative particle, زو = 'from him/her/it'). ننگ is 'shame/disgrace' and یابد is 3rd‑person present/subjunctive/epic form of 'find/attain' (here 'will find/attain'); گهر is 'the gem' — the clause reads 'from whom the gem will find shame' i.e. 'by whom the gem will suffer disgrace.'
segments.s244-g25: بدان here is the compound adverbial/particle formed from the prefix ب- + دان (an older/decorative locative/contrastive word) meaning roughly 'to/thereupon'; تا introduces a purpose clause and is followed by the subjunctive/optative ببینم ('that I may see'). یکی modifies روی (an indefinite/numercial 'one/a certain'), and روی شاه is the genitival idea 'the king's face' with the ezāfe understood rather than written (poetic spelling drops the -e).
segments.s244-g26: نمایم is 1st-person singular present-subjunctive ('I show' / 'I will show' in purpose/result clauses); بدو is the clitic prepositional phrase 'to him'; اختر نیک راه is a noun phrase 'star (اختر) + auspicious (نیک) + path/way (راه)' where نیک works as an evaluative adjective and the whole means 'the auspicious star's way' or 'the auspicious path of the star.' Word order is noun + adjective + noun in this poetic line.
segments.s244-g27: بکاخ اندر آمد is a simple verbal clause: بکاخ (to/at the palace) + اندر 'in/into' + آمد 'came/entered' — standard prepositional phrase + verb order. پرستارفش is a single lexical item in the manuscript whose reading/meaning is unclear here (the gloss lists it as 'obscure/uncertain'), so the line likely preserves a corrupted or rare word.
segments.s244-g28: بر شاه is a prepositional phrase 'toward/on the king'; با دست is the instrumental 'with (his) hand'; کرده is a perfect participle 'having done/with (it) done' that links to the following verbal form; بکش here is a simple verbal form meaning 'draw/strike/pull' (classical narrational forms often use such bare stems). The sequence literally reads 'toward the king, with (his) hand having (done), he drew/struck.'
segments.s244-g29: بیامد is the past tense 'he came'; دمان (an older spelling of دامن) here means 'hem/edge' or 'skirt' and functions adverbially as a location; تا introduces an extent 'up to' and is followed by بنزدیک 'near/close to' modifying تخت 'throne' — so the clause means 'he came to the hem and (came) up close to the throne.'
segments.s244-g30: بر here introduces the target of the action 'toward/on' (بر افراسیاب = 'to/for Afrasiab'); آفرین is an exclamation/noun 'praise, acclamation' used as content ('he uttered praise'); کرد is the past auxiliary/verb 'made/did' so آفرین کرد = 'he praised/applauded'; سخت is an adverb 'strongly/heartily' modifying کرد. Word order is noun/target → object/exclamation → verb → adverb.
segments.s244-g31: همی بود is the habitual/past-state construction 'he was (always/there)'; در پیش تختش is a prepositional phrase 'in/at the front/in the presence of his throne' (تختش = 'his throne'); بپای is an older poetic verb form meaning 'at the foot/of the feet' or 'to abide/stand by' — together the clause says he 'stood at the foot of/fore of the throne.'
segments.s244-g32: چو is a comparative/simile particle 'as/like'; دستور here means 'order/arrangement' or more idiomatically 'attendant/one who is in order' in older registers; پاکیزه 'pure/clean/proper' and رهنمای 'guide/adviser' are a coordinated noun phrase describing the manner/role — 'as an orderly, clean (proper) and guiding attendant.' The line uses adjectives/nouns in sequence without an explicit copula (typical of poetic Persian).
segments.s244-g73: This phrase sets a circumstantial frame: بآرام (an adverb meaning 'quietly/gently') comes first for emphasis; بر + کینه‌جویی ('on/upon vengeful-seeking') indicates the sphere or manner in which the action happens. همی is the archaic/progressive particle marking continuous or habitual aspect that carries over to the verb in the next segment.
segments.s244-g74: This clause contains the main verb phrase. گل زهر 'the poisonous flower' is the object phrase (گل = flower, زهر = poison modifying it). خیره is an adverbial/adjectival state ('dazed/staring'), and ببویی is the classical present/imperfect form (prefixed ب- with the stem بوییدن) meaning 'you smell' or 'you will smell' in a continuous/habitual sense; the trailing همی again reinforces the ongoing aspect.
segments.s244-g75: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). خون بیژن is the genitive phrase 'Bijan's blood' (خون = blood, بیژن = proper name). بریزی is the second-person present/subjunctive (with b- prefix) 'you pour/shear/let fall'. بر + ین here spell out برین (بر این) = 'on/onto this' or 'here', so the verb's direction/target is given at the end.
segments.s244-g76: ز (poetic for از) marks origin or source 'from'. توران is the place-name 'Turan'. برآید is an archaic/present form meaning 'will arise/appear' (3rd‑person). همان 'that same' points back to the thing arising, and گرد کین is a compound image ('گرد' here figuratively = dust/storm/host, paired with کین = vengeance) meaning roughly 'the same storm/host of vengeance'.
segments.s244-g77: This is a vocative/comment clause: خردمند can be read as an address 'wise one' or as an epithet 'wise' applied to شاهی ('king'), and و ما کهترا follows as a contrast 'and we are inferior/lesser'. The word order is compact and poetic: the speaker puts the praise/appeal and the self‑deprecating contrast side by side.
segments.s244-g78: This is a pair of imperatives addressed to 'تو' (you). چشم خرد is a genitive compound 'the eye of wisdom' (چشم = sight/eye, خرد = wisdom). باز کن is the periphrastic imperative 'open' (باز = open; کن is the light verbal auxiliary/imperative), and بنگرا is a second imperative 'look!' — the two verbs form a coordinated command: 'open the eye of wisdom and look.'
segments.s244-g79: نگه کن is an imperative 'look/observe' (نگه = gaze/watch + کن auxiliary imperative). ازان = 'from that' or 'of that' (poetic form of از + آن). کین = 'vengeance/hatred', and که introduces a relative clause; گستردیا is an archaic past (3rd person) 'he spread/extended' (the -یا ending is a classical past marker), so the whole segment means 'Look at that vengeance which he spread.'
segments.s244-g80: This is a questioning/exclamatory clause. ابا here is ambiguous in isolation (not the common verb sense in modern lists) and likely a proper name or an archaic word in context; شاه ایران = 'the king of Iran'; چه is an exclamatory 'what/what then'; بر خوردیا is an archaic verbal phrase ('برخورد' in classical usage = 'to meet/encounter/happen to') in past tense — the whole asks 'what then happened to the Iranian king when he encountered it?'. Because some dictionary senses supplied in the metadata do not fit the classical usages here, indexes of impossible matches are given as -1 below.
segments.s244-g49: مرا ... نیست here expresses possession/need with an object-pronoun + negated copula. م + را together function as the older spelling of مرا ('me' / 'for me'); این نیاز is the noun phrase 'this need'. از در خویش is a prepositional phrase 'from his door/court' (در = 'door/gate', خویش = 'his own'); نیست is the negated existential/copular verb 'is not'. Word order is typical older Persian: (to me) this need from his door is not — i.e. 'I have no need of his court.'
segments.s244-g50: کس از کهتران تو درویش نیست = 'No one of your lower-born is a dervish.' کس is an indefinite/negative 'no one' here; از introduces the partitive/among ('of/among'); کهتران = 'the lower-born/younger/meaner people' (the plural group being referred to); تو is possessive 'your' (in classical usage the pronoun can follow the group); درویش is the predicate noun 'dervish/poor ascetic'; نیست negates the copula. Word order: Subject/partitive + possessor + predicate + negation.
segments.s244-g51: بداند here functions as a 3rd-person present/imperative sense 'let him consider / may he deem' (classical usage can be hortative or optative), followed by the subject شهنشاه 'the shah' and the attributive برترمنش 'high-/proud-minded'. The verb precedes its subject, which is common in older/persian poetic word order.
segments.s244-g52: ستوده is a predicate adjective 'praised'. بهر کار is a prepositional phrase 'for/with respect to (every) deed'—بهر here = 'for' or 'with regard to'. بی‌سرزنش is an adjectival phrase 'without reproach / blameless'. Together they qualify the high-minded person: 'praised, for his deeds, blameless.'
segments.s244-g53: که introduces a content clause 'that'. من شاه را is the speaker + direct object marker (I to the shah / I to the king); پیش ازین is a temporal phrase 'before this / previously' (ین is the orthographic/poetic variant of این); چند بار 'several times'. Word order places the time and frequency after the object.
segments.s244-g54: همی دادمی is an archaic/habitual past construction: همی (iterative/continuous adverb) + دادمی (old 1st- or 3rd-person past form of 'to give') yields 'I used to give / I have often given'. پند is 'advice' and بر چند کار means 'on several matters' (بر = 'on/regarding'). The whole clause means 'I have often given advice to the shah on several affairs.'
segments.s244-g55: بفرمان من is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'by my command'. هیچ negates 'none'. نامد فراز is an older/poetic expression: نامد here relates to 'name/fame' (i.e. 'became named' / 'won renown') and فراز 'above/higher'—together meaning 'rose above in fame' or 'attained higher renown'. The clause thus says 'By my command, none rose above him in fame.'
segments.s244-g56: ازو داشتم is 'I had from him' (ازو = 'from him/it'); داشتم is the past imperfect/past-tense 'I had' (possession/authority). کارها is the object 'the affairs/matters'. دست باز is an idiomatic nominal phrase 'a free hand' (دست = 'power/control', باز = 'open/free'). Word order places the possessed object before the nominal predicate 'دست باز'.
segments.s244-g161: A compact noun phrase: the proper name منیژه (Manijeh) is followed by the adjective برهنه (“naked”). بیک here is a contracted/poetic form of به‑ + آن or بی‑ (i.e. “without”) linking to چادرا (chador/veil), so the phrase means “Manijeh, naked, without a chador/veil.” Word order (name + adjective) is normal in Persian poetry, and the object/privative element is compressed for metrical reasons.
segments.s244-g162: Continuation of the description: برهنه دو پای is a verbless nominal phrase meaning “with (her) two feet bare” (adjective + numeral + noun). و links the next phrase; گشاده سرا is an idiomatic/poetic compound meaning the upper garment or chest is open/loose — literally “open‑sara,” i.e. her robe/open bodice. The sentence omits any copula and strings descriptive phrases for vivid effect.
segments.s244-g163: کشیدش is a past transitive verb with the object suffix ‑ش (“her”) — “they pulled/dragged her.” دوان is an adverbial/participle meaning “running/rushing,” describing the manner. تا ... indicates motion destination (“as far as / to”), and here بدان is the fused form of به + آن (“to that”), followed by چاهسار (a place name/descriptor: “well‑field”). The sequence expresses motion + destination in compact poetic order.
segments.s244-g164: A pair of verbless descriptive phrases. دو دیده پر از خون uses the pattern (NUM + noun) + پر از + N to mean “her two eyes were full of blood.” The conjunction و links رخ جویبار, where رخ (“face”) is qualified by جویبار (a metaphorical ‘stream’); the copula is omitted, so this reads as “and (her) face a stream,” i.e. streaming/flowing (poetic imagery).
segments.s244-g165: بدو گفت is a light, formulaic sequence: بدو = “to her/to him” (dative) and گفت is the past “said” (the lexical list given mislabels گفت; here it functions as ‘said’). اینک = “now.” ترا is the older/poetic order of تو + را (“you” + object marker), so the clause so far is “he said to her: ‘now to you...’.” خان و مان is an archaic binomial meaning roughly “house and home/abode” (poetic pairing), treated as the theme of the sentence.
segments.s244-g166: زواری qualifies the following phrase and is best read as related to pilgrimage (either ‘pilgrim’s’ or ‘as a pilgrim’). برین is the fused form بر + این (on/at this), so برین بسته means “bound upon/at this (place).” بسته is a past participle/adjective “bound/tied,” and تا جاودان is a temporal/completive phrase “until / for ever.” Altogether: “(your) house and home are bound to this place as (that of) a pilgrim, forever.”
segments.s244-g167: Subject + verb + locative: غریوان is ambiguous here (it can be a proper name or a poetic noun — the lexical senses listed are ‘neckline/bosom’ but in context the word functions as a subject, likely a proper name or epithet); همی is the frequentative/adverbial particle “still/continually,” and گشت (past of گشتن) means “went/roamed/turned.” بر گرد دشت = “around the plain” (بر گرد = around, دشت = plain).
segments.s244-g168: Temporal clause introduced by چو (“when/as”): چو یک روز و یک شب gives the span “one day and one night.” برو بر گذشت is a poetic, somewhat pleonastic verbal sequence meaning roughly “went by / passed” (برو and بر both contribute a motion sense before گذشت “passed”), so the whole clause means “as one day and one night passed.”
segments.s244-g57: مکش is a negative imperative (archaic/poetic) meaning “do not …”; here it introduces the injunction. گفتمت is a past tense verb + clitic (گفتم + -ت) “I told you.” پور کاوس را follows as the addressed person or the content of what was told; پور = “son/descendant,” کاوس is the king’s name, and را is the definite/object marker marking that noun phrase as definite (here functioning as direct object or vocative in epic diction).
segments.s244-g58: که introduces a subordinate clause meaning “that / (so) that,” here giving the content of the warning. دشمن is the noun “enemy” used as a complement to the verb کنی; کنی is the present/subjunctive (or jussive) 2nd‑person form of کردن “to make/cause” (here “you make / cause to be”). رستم و طوس are the definite objects (names) joined by و and the final را marks them as definite direct objects.
segments.s244-g59: کز ایران is a poetic prepositional phrase: کز (= از) + ایران = “from Iran.” بپیلان is a poetic/instrumental compound involving پیل (elephant) with a prefixed ب- meaning roughly “with/by elephants.” بکوبندمان is 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive (ب‑ prefix + کوبند) plus the object clitic -مان “us” — used here to express potential/future action “they strike/pound us.”
segments.s244-g60: ز هم is “from/away from one another” or “from together/among us” (ز = poetic 'from', هم = 'together/one another'). بگسلانند is 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive (ب‑ + گسلانند) meaning “they break/tear apart,” and پیوندمان is پیوند “bond/tie” with the pronominal suffix -مان “our,” i.e. “our bond(s).”
segments.s244-g61: This is a noun + relative clause: سیاوش is the name; که بود is a relative “who was” (که introducing the clause, بود = past 'was'); از نژاد کیان = “of the lineage/stock of the Kians” (از = of/from, نژاد = lineage, کیان = the Kian royal house).
segments.s244-g62: ز بهر تو is a prepositional phrase “for your sake” (ز بهر = poetic for “for/for the sake of,” تو = you). بسته کمر is a compound participial/adjectival phrase: بسته (past participle “bound/girt”) + کمر (waist/girdle) — together meaning “having girded his loins.” بر میان here gives the locative/figurative position “about the middle / at his loins” (بر = on/upon, میان = middle).
segments.s244-g63: بکشتی here reads as a circumstantial phrase “by ship / in a ship” (ب‑ + کشتی) rather than the verb 'you killed' (context shows movement). بخیره is an adverb “safely/securely.” سیاوش را is the definite object (the person transported) marked by را. The order is poetic but means “you sent/put Siyavash safely on a ship.”
segments.s244-g64: بزهر is an instrumental/with‑phrase centered on زهر “poison” (here “with poison”); اندر = “into/in” and آمیختی is the 2nd‑person past tense “you mixed” (آمیخت + -ی). نوش is the noun “drink,” and را is the definite/object marker: “you mixed poison into his drink.”
segments.s244-g81: هم is an emphatic/adverbial 'also/too' placed before the object آنرا ('that'); همی is the archaic/prose particle marking continued or habitual action before the verb; خواستار here is a participial/adjectival stem 'desiring/seeking'; آوری is the 2nd‑person present form of آوردن 'to bring/produce' (Ferdowsi uses -ی for the present). Together the clause literally reads 'you continually are-desirer that' → idiomatically 'you keep asking/desiring that'.
segments.s244-g82: درخت بلا is a genitive construction 'tree of calamity' (epithet); را is the definite/object marker attaching to the phrase; ببار is an archaic/periphrastic verb (from باریدن/باران) meaning 'to pour/bring down' and here is followed by آوری, again 2nd‑person present of آوردن functioning as the main verbal form. The two verbs form a poetic compound meaning 'you cause to pour/downfall' → 'you bring down' or 'you shower (calamity)'.
segments.s244-g83: چو here is the temporal/concessive 'when' introducing the subordinate clause; کینه دو is a compact expression meaning 'enmity/grudge that becomes twofold/mutual' (دو after a noun often implies doubling or reciprocity in older Persian); گردد is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of گشتن 'to become/turn'; نداریم پای places the negated verb نداریم ('we do not have') before its noun پای ('foot/firm footing'), an idiomatic order meaning 'we have no standing/support'.
segments.s244-g84: ایا is a question/introductory particle (here rhetorical, 'are you...? / then?'); پهلوان جهان کدخدای is a string of epithets in apposition: پهلوان 'champion/hero', جهان 'of the world' (an attributive epithet), and کدخدای 'kādkhodā' used honorifically here as 'chieftain/headman'. Word order is nominal apposition, asking rhetorically whether the addressee is this exalted champion/head.
segments.s244-g85: This is a classical inverted comparative: به از تو ('better than you') precedes the negative verb نداند ('knows/considers not') and the subject کسی ('anyone/no one'). The literal order 'better-than-you knows no one Giv‑ra' yields the idiomatic meaning 'No one considers Giv better than you.' را marks Giv as the definite/contrastive name.
segments.s244-g86: نهنگ بلا is a compound epithet ('whale/giant of calamity' — a poetic way to call someone a great bringer of disaster or a mighty calamity). It is placed before the proper name رستم as a descriptive epithet; نیو را that follows is an additional name/epithet with را as object/contrast marker. The clause is nominal/appositive: 'Rostam, the n hen g of calamity.'
segments.s244-g87: چو again is 'when' introducing a temporal clause; a sequence of names follows in apposition: گودرز (Goudarz, personal name), کشواد (Keshvad — here treated as a name/title), and پولادچنگ ('steel‑clawed' — a descriptive epithet attached to the name). Poetic Persian often strings names and epithets without additional linking verbs.
segments.s244-g88: که here is a relative/subordinating particle 'who/that' introducing the action clause; آید is 3rd‑person singular present of آمدن 'comes'; ز بهر is the set phrase 'for the sake of/on behalf of' (ز = poetic 'from', بهر = 'for'); نبیره means 'offspring/scion' (the person on whose behalf he fights); بجنگ is the infinitival/construct form 'to fight' (ب‑ + جنگ), expressing purpose or motion into battle.
segments.s244-g113: ازو is the old/poetic prepositional form meaning 'from him' and introduces the source of advice. پند is the noun 'advice/counsel.' گیرند is 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive (classical Persian form) used here optatively/exhortatively — 'may they take' or 'let them take.' ایرانیان is the plural subject ('the Iranians') and is the understood subject of گیرند.
segments.s244-g114: نبندند is ن + بندند, a present/subjunctive 3rd-person-plural form with negative sense 'may they not bind/put.' The adverbial phrase ازین پس (از + ین (orthographic variant of این) + پس) means 'from now on/after this.' بدی را is the definite object 'evil' plus the object marker را. میان here means 'between' or 'in the middle'; together the clause says the (same) subject should not 'put/establish evil between' (people) henceforth — the subject is carried over from the previous segment.
segments.s244-g115: چنان is an adverb 'thus/so.' کرد is the simple past 3rd-person singular 'did/acted.' سالار is the noun 'commander/chief' (the subject). کو is an archaic/poetic relative/connector (here functioning like 'who/that'); it does not match the modern sense 'where' and is used to introduce the relative clause 'who saw the counsel.' رای is 'counsel/plan' and دید is the past 'saw/perceived.' Note the verb–subject inversion (چنان کرد سالار) is a common poetic order; the relative clause follows.
segments.s244-g116: دلش is دل + enclitic possessive ش 'his heart' (subject of دید). با here means 'by/with' and introduces the means: با زبان شاه 'by the king's tongue/words' (زبان used as 'speech/utterance'). بر جای is a locative phrase 'to/in the place' or 'in its proper seat'; دید is past 'saw/perceived.' Grammatically this is a simple past clause: 'his heart perceived, by the king's words, the place.'
segments.s244-g117: ز is the poetic/prepositional form 'from/by' indicating source or cause. دستور here means 'command/order' or 'ordinance.' پاکیزه is an adjective 'pure/clean' modifying دستور. راهبر is the agent 'guide/leader' whose command is meant; the whole phrase z + N modifies what follows (shows cause/source).
segments.s244-g118: درفشان is a compound nominal/adjectival word meaning 'shining standard/banner' (or 'standard-bearer'); شود is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive/passive auxiliary 'will become / will be made / will happen.' شاه is the logical beneficiary/possessor ('the king'). بر گاه (بر + گاه) is a locative 'upon the place/occasion' and here means 'upon the throne/seat'; the final بر is a poetic repetition/emphasis of the preposition. The clause thus expresses a future/result: 'the shining standard will come to be for the king upon the throne.'
segments.s244-g119: بگرسیوز is an archaic/obscure verb form (not standard modern Persian) that functions here as an adverbial/participial element or clause-head; its exact lexical sense is uncertain in isolation and is best read as a poetic prefatory verb/phrase. آنگه is 'then/thereupon.' بفرمود is the honorific past/polite verb 'he commanded/said' (of the king). شاه is the subject 'the king.'
segments.s244-g120: که introduces a subordinate clause reported after 'he commanded' (i.e. 'that ...'). بند here is 'fetter/bond' (object), گران is the adjective 'heavy/weighty' modifying بند, and ساز is an imperative/verb 'make' (classical poetic usage; here the verb is truncated to ساز rather than بساز). و is 'and.' تاریک چاه is a noun phrase 'a dark pit/well.' Taken together the clause is the king's command: 'Make heavy fetters and (a) dark pit.'
segments.s244-g1: ببخشود is an archaic/past 3rd‑person form of بخشیدن and here functions as a simple past verb 'granted/ bestowed'. The subject یزدان follows the verb (typical in older Persian poetry) and جوانیش (جوان + enclitic ـش = 'his youth/vigor') is the definite direct object marked by را. The order is Verb — Subject — Object with را flagging the object.
segments.s244-g2: بهم here is an adverb meaning 'together/altogether' or 'in confusion' modifying the verb برشکست ('shattered/broke'); برشکست is a past tense verbal form meaning 'shattered/ broke'. آن گمانیش is a noun phrase 'that belief/expectation of his' (گمان + enclitic ـش) with را marking it as definite object. Word order places the adverb before the verb and the object at the end.
segments.s244-g3: کننده is an agentive present participle 'the doer/maker' used as a noun; همی + کند is an iterative/presentive construction ('still/continually does' or 'keeps doing') where همی marks continuity and کند is the present/presentive verb 'does/makes'. جای درخت is the object 'place/spot of the tree' (noun + modifier). The literal order is '-maker still makes place (of) tree.'
segments.s244-g4: پدید آمد is a two‑word verbal phrase meaning 'appeared' (پدید = 'visible/manifest' + آمد = 'came'), with the phrase functioning as a past action. از دور is a locative 'from afar', پیران is the plural subject 'the elders', and ز بخت ('by fate' or 'through fortune') is an ablative/causal phrase indicating manner or cause. The verb phrase appears before or adjacent to its arguments in this poetic ordering.
segments.s244-g5: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. پیران ویسه is a genitive/attributive sequence 'the elders of Viseh' (pیران = elders, ویسه = name). بدانجا is the locative adverb 'to that place/there', and رسید is the simple past 'arrived/reached'. The clause is a temporal subordinate: 'When the elders of Viseh reached that place...'.
segments.s244-g6: همه راه is a phrase 'the whole road/entire way' (همه modifies راه). ترک کمربسته is a noun plus participial adjective 'Turks girded' (کمربسته literally 'girt/girded', i.e. ready/armed). دید is the past tense 'saw' (here agreeing implicitly with the elders). The natural reading is 'they saw Turks, girded, along the whole road.'
segments.s244-g7: یکی is an indefinite 'one/a certain'. دار here (in this context) functions as a vertical post/gallows/tree; the sense 'gallows' or 'pole' fits the scene. برپای کرده is a compound participial expression 'having set up / erected' (برپای = 'standing/erect', کرده = past participle 'made/put'), and بلند is the adjective 'tall/high' modifying دار. The noun phrase order is 'a certain gallows erected tall.'
segments.s244-g8: کمندی is 'a lasso/snare'. برو here is an archaic contraction of بر او ('upon it' / 'on that'), a poetic locative; in other words it means 'on it' rather than the modern verb 'go' (so treat it as a prepositional phrase). بسته is a past participle 'tied/bound' describing the lasso, and چون پای بند is a simile 'like a foot‑shackle' (چون = like/as, پای = foot, بند = fetter/bond). The whole phrase reads 'a lasso tied upon it like a fetter on a foot.'
segments.s244-g137: برو is an imperative 'go!' addressing someone. با سواران is a prepositional phrase ('with the horsemen') indicating accompaniment placed after the imperative. و links a second imperative clause; تاراج کن is formed from the noun تاراج + the light verb کن used imperatively, yielding 'pillage!' (literally 'do plunder').
segments.s244-g138: نگون‌بخت را is the definite/direct object marked with را ('the ill-fated one' / 'that unfortunate person' as object). بی‌سر و تاج کن is a coordinated result/complement to کن: the light verb کن takes the following predicates ('make/headless and crownless') — literally 'make him headless and (take) his crown' (imperative). Word order places the object before its adjectival/complement phrases for emphasis.
segments.s244-g139: بگو is the imperative 'say' introducing reported speech. ای marks a vocative address; بنفرین here is used as a vocative 'accursed!' (addressing Fate) rather than the verb 'to curse'. شوریده بخت is a noun phrase 'distraught/maddened Fate' used in apposition—together the vocative yields 'O accursed, distraught Fate.'
segments.s244-g140: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that...') explaining what is to be said. بر تو is the prepositional complement 'upon you'; نزیبد (literary present) means 'befits' or 'is fitting' (here used impersonally: 'does not befit you' in context). همی is an archaic emphatic/adverb meaning 'ever/always'. تاج و تخت are coordinated nouns 'crown and throne', the items said not to befit the addressee.
segments.s244-g141: به ننگ is an adverbial phrase 'with shame' or 'to disgrace'. از introduces the source/agent ('from/of') and here links to کیان پست = 'the base/despicable of the Kians' (poetic adjective after the noun). کردی is the second-person past 'you did/made'; سرم (my head) is the direct object meaning 'my honor/my head' used metaphorically for the speaker's dignity (no matching sense provided, see senses).
segments.s244-g142: بخاک اندر انداختی is a compound predicative phrase: بخاک 'to the ground/into the dirt' + اندر 'in/into' (poetic reinforcement) + انداختی 'you threw/cast' (past). افسرم 'my afsar' is the direct object — here 'my helmet/crown' (ancient sense); the whole means 'you cast my helmet to the ground.'
segments.s244-g143: برهنه is an adjective 'naked' describing the person to be moved. کشانش is a verbal participle/stem 'dragging' with the enclitic object -ش ('him'); ببر is the imperative 'take!' or 'carry off!'. تا here introduces the destination 'to' or 'as far as' and بچاه is a fused prepositional/locative expression 'to the well' (ب- + چاه).
segments.s244-g144: که begins a relative clause ('which/that') modifying 'the well' (بچاه). در چاه is the locative 'in the well'. بین here is the verbal root 'to see' (classical usage within the relative clause), and آنک دیدی بگاه — 'which you saw at that moment' — provides the temporal/identifying remark (آنک 'which/that', دیدی 'you saw' past, بگاه 'at the time/at that moment').
segments.s244-g121: دو دستش = numeral + noun + enclitic pronoun: دو (two) modifies دست (hand) and ش is the enclitic 'his' attached to دست, so 'his two hands'. بزنجیر is the prepositional/contracted construction be+زنجیر meaning 'with/in chains' (the prefix be attached to the noun). و گردن بغل links with و ('and') and a noun phrase گردن بغل: گردن 'neck' + بغل ('embrace/side'), here functioning as a compound meaning a collar/embrace around the neck.
segments.s244-g122: یکی بند رومی بکردار مل is a compact descriptive phrase: یکی بند = 'one/a single fetter/bond'; رومی modifies بند as an adjective 'Roman/Byzantine'; the following words (بکردار مل) are epithets/appellative in apposition — بکردار functions as an adjective/epithet and مل ('realm') further characterizes the fetter as belonging to or notable for the realm. Word order is noun + adjectives/epithets following it, typical in classical Persian.
segments.s244-g123: ببندش is the 2nd‑person imperative 'bind him' (ببند + enclitic ـش for 'him'). به here introduces the instrument and functions as 'with/by' (becoming 'with' the following instrument). مسمار آهنگران is the instrument phrase: مسمار 'nail/peg' modified by آهنگران 'blacksmiths' (genitive/attributive), so 'an iron peg of the smiths'.
segments.s244-g124: ز سر تا = a frozen poetic prepositional span meaning 'from (the) head to ...'; ز is poetic 'from', سر 'head', تا 'as far as/until'. بپایش is an enclitic/contracted form corresponding to به پایش ('to his foot(s)'); it marks the lower limit. ببند is another imperative 'bind/fasten'. اندرَان is the locative adverb 'in that/therein' (an older form meaning 'inside it'). The whole sequence gives the instruction 'bind him from head to foot inside there.'
segments.s244-g125: چو بستی = temporal clause 'when/after you have bound' (چو = 'when', بستی here perfect/past sense 'you bound/you have bound'). نگون is an adjective 'ill‑fated/doomed' modifying the prisoner. اندر + بچاه = اندر ('in/into') + بچاه ('into the well') where بچاه is the verbalized/contracted prepositional phrase meaning 'cast/put into the well'; together they mean 'cast the ill‑fated into the well.'
segments.s244-g126: چو introduces another result/temporal clause 'so that/when'. بی‌بهره is an adjectival predicate 'deprived, cut off'. گردد is the 3rd‑person present/subjunctive of گردیدن 'become' (here 'become/cut off'). ز is the poetic 'from' and خورشید و ماه are the coordinated nouns 'sun and moon' (i.e. light). The clause states the intended consequence: 'so that he becomes deprived of the sun and moon.'
segments.s244-g127: ببر here is the imperative of بردن 'take/bring' (command form). پیل is the direct object 'elephant'. و connects items. آن functions as the demonstrative 'that' modifying the noun phrase سنگ اکوان دیو: سنگ 'stone' modified by اکوان 'of the worlds' and then the noun دیو 'demon/monster'. The phrase names a particular monstrous stone being.
segments.s244-g128: که is the relative pronoun 'which/that'. از is the preposition 'from/out of'. ژرف دریای گیهان is a genitival chain: ژرف 'deep' modifies دریای (the construct form of دریا 'sea'), and گیهان 'world' specifies which sea ('the deep sea of the world'). خدیو follows as an epithet/title (here used as a descriptive name/epithet of the being or its origin). The whole relative clause qualifies the previous monster: 'which is from the deep sea of the world (— the khedive/so‑called ruler).'
segments.s244-g41: چو + past verb introduces a temporal clause: چو بشنید = 'when (they) heard'. پیران is the plural noun 'elders' and the following phrase خسرو پرست is a postpositive modifier meaning 'Khosrow‑worshipping' (i.e. 'who adored Khosrow'). The verb بشنید is a past third‑person form referring back to پیران.
segments.s244-g42: زمین را is a definite direct object marked by the enclitic را ('the ground'). ببوسید is a past action 'kissed' (3rd person). و simply coordinates two verbs, and بر پای جست is a short verbal phrase meaning 'sprang to (his) feet' — بر (on/upon) + پای (foot) + جست (past of 'to leap').
segments.s244-g43: بادا is an optative particle meaning 'may it be', so جاوید بادا 'may (it) be eternal'. که here introduces the clause (classical/poetic 'that/may it be that'). ت + را together form ترا (the old/poetic object/pronoun 'to you'), so ترا بخت و جای = 'your fortune and your place/position.' Word order places the wished-upon items after the optative.
segments.s244-g44: مبادا is a negative optative 'may it not be / lest'. ز تخت تو = 'from your throne' (ز = 'from', تخت = 'throne', تو = 'your'). پردخته جای is a poetic phrase in which پردخته functions as a descriptive/participial modifier of جای ('place'); the whole phrase expresses the wish that no place/seat be taken away from (or torn off) your throne.
segments.s244-g45: ز شاهان گیتی places the domain/measure first: 'among/from the kings of the world'. ستایش تراست is the predicate: ستایش 'praise' + تراست = ترا + است 'is yours', i.e. 'praise belongs to you' (inversion of modern Persian order is common in poetry).
segments.s244-g46: ز خورشید برتر sets a comparison: 'higher/superior to the sun' (ز = 'from/of' in poetic use). نمایش تراست = 'your display is' (نمایش 'display/splendor' + تراست 'is yours'). The comparative adjective برتر precedes the noun it qualifies, as is frequent in classical verse.
segments.s244-g47: مرا را here functions as the dative/accusative pronoun 'to me' (poetic splitting of مرا into م + را is shown in the edition). هرچ = 'whatever/anything', باید is a modal 'ought/should' (here 'that is needed'). The phrase should be understood as 'Whatever I need (هرچ باید) is in your fortune' with هست as the copula at the end of the clause. (In classical word order the copula and possessive relationship appear after the noun phrase.)
segments.s244-g48: ز مردان وز گنج و نیروی دست is a list of sources: ز = 'from/of', مردان = 'men' (here meaning followers/retainers or heroes), وز in this poetic context functions as a contraction/combination meaning 'and from' (classical usage 'و' + 'ز'), followed by گنج 'treasure' and نیروی دست 'the strength/power of (the) hand' — i.e. resources provided by men, treasure, and military/physical power.
segments.s244-g89: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. برزد is an archaic past/punctual verb meaning 'fell/struck' (here: 'struck/struck down'); the grammatical subject (the water) follows in classical order. بران = بر + آن, an older/poetic locative 'upon that'; تیز modifies آب and here means 'swift' (a common epic epithet for moving water).
segments.s244-g90: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. داد is the simple past 'gave' and takes پاسخ ('answer') as its object; placing the object before the verb is common in older poetic prose. پس = 'then' (temporal), and افراسیاب is the proper-name subject placed after the verb (inverted/poetic word order).
segments.s244-g91: The initial که here introduces the clause ('that'/'do you not see'); بیژن is vocative or topical address. نبینی is 2nd-person present/imperative-negative form used rhetorically 'do you not see' (classical rhetorical question). The second که introduces the subordinate content clause 'با من چه کرد' = 'what he did to/with me', where کرد is simple past 'did'.
segments.s244-g92: بایران و توران here functions as a prepositional/locative phrase meaning 'before/to Iran and Turan' — in epic Persian the preposition به is often compressed to بـ/بـا form in manuscripts; شدم is 1st-person past 'I became'. روی زرد is the predicate 'yellow-faced' (i.e. 'turned pale'); adjective follows the noun in the usual Persian order.
segments.s244-g93: نبینی again is the 2nd-person negative present used rhetorically 'do you not see'. کزین = ک + ز + این, a fused demonstrative/prepositional 'from this'. بدهنر is an adjectival epithet 'ill-bred / poorly taught' modifying دخترم; the possessive suffix -م on دختر marks 'my daughter'.
segments.s244-g94: چه is exclamatory here ('what a…'); رسوایی 'disgrace' is the noun that follows. آمد is the simple past 'came' (impersonal/patient construction 'a disgrace came'). بپیران = بِ + پیران ('to/among the elders') is a dative/locative phrase, and سرم in context means 'my head' (my reputation/honor); note the lemma senses provided do not reflect this common meaning, so the head/possessive reading is used here.
segments.s244-g95: همان is the demonstrative 'that/the same'. نام پوشیده = 'hidden name' or 'concealed fame' (noun + adjective). رویان here is read as a proper name/epithet attached to من ('my Ruyan' or 'my epithet'); alternatively رویان can mean 'sprouts' but context favors a name/epithet. The possessive من attaches after the noun to mean 'my...'.
segments.s244-g96: ز is the classical preposition 'from'. پرده 'curtain/veil' is the source phrase. بگسترد is an older/past form 'he spread' (بـ- prefix plus گسترد), and بر انجمن = 'upon/before the assembly' (بر + انجمن) indicates the target/audience; word order here is inverted in poetic style (verb before subject omitted).
segments.s244-g17: بپرسید is a past tense narrative verb (“he asked”). و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” گفتش is the verb گفت plus the enclitic -ش (to him), “he said to him.” که introduces the reported question/clause. چون آمدی is an interrogative subordinate clause: چون here means “how” and آمدی is the 2nd-person past “you came/arrived.” Together the clause functions as what he said/asked.
segments.s244-g18: از ایران is a simple prepositional phrase “from Iran.” همانا is an emphatic particle meaning “indeed/truly.” بخون آمدی is a poetic expression: بخون is here the prefixed بِ‑ + خون (“with blood”), and آمدی is “you came,” so the phrase means “you came covered/involved with blood” (i.e. bloodied). This بخون is not the imperative verb “recite” but a prepositional combination.
segments.s244-g19: همه داستان بیژن is a noun phrase placed before the verb and means “the whole story of Bijan” (همه = entire; داستان = story; بیژن = Bijan). او را is the object/topic marked with را plus the pronoun او “him” (recipient of the telling). بگفت is past narrative “(he) told.” Word order is topic/object before the verb, typical in classical Persian narration.
segments.s244-g20: چنانچون is a manner adverb “just as/just like.” رسیدش is رسید + enclitic -ش “it reached him/it came to him” (past). ز is the poetic form of از “from.” بدخواه جفت is a terse compound: بدخواه “ill‑wisher/malicious” modifying جفت “pair,” i.e. ‘the ill‑wishing pair’ or ‘the malicious pair’ from whom the news came; the whole segment states the manner/source of how the story reached him.
segments.s244-g21: ببخشود is past tense “he forgave/pardoned.” پیران ویسه here functions as the proper name Piran Viseh (a character), i.e. the subject doing the pardoning. بروی is a verb-form here meaning “(he) let (him) go / departed” (an archaic narrative verb), so the whole clause says that Piran Viseh pardoned and released/let go.
segments.s244-g22: ز مژگان is an ablative phrase “from (his) eyelashes.” سرشکش is a small drop/teardrop (سرشک = droplet), فرو is an adverb “down(ward),” and شد is the past auxiliary “fell/became,” so فرو شد = “fell down.” بروی here is the poetic contraction of بر روی (“upon/on his face”), so the final phrase means the teardrop fell down upon his face.
segments.s244-g23: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past narrative verb “he commanded/ordered.” تا here expresses duration/extent “for/until,” and یک زمانش is a duration phrase “one period/moment” (زمان + possessive/enclitic ش used idiomatically). بدار is an imperative or jussive meaning “keep/detain” (order to hold someone) — together: “He ordered, ‘Detain him for one period.’”
segments.s244-g24: نکردند is the past negative “they did not (do it).” و is “and.” گفتا is a narrative past “they said.” هم ایدر بدار is reported speech: هم “also,” ایدر (ایدر) = “here” (archaic), and بدار again the imperative/jussive “keep/detain (him).” So they say, “Also keep him here.”
segments.s244-g65: بدیدی is an older/poetic second-person singular past tense of دیدن ("you saw"). بدیهای is بدی ("evil, wrongdoing") with the plural suffix های; it functions as the direct object. ایرانیان follows to mark whose evils these are (genitive/attributive relationship). Word order here places the verb before its object for poetic emphasis.
segments.s244-g66: که is the relative/conjunction "that" introducing what was done. کردند is third-person plural past of کردن ("they did"). با here has the sense of acting toward/against ("against, with respect to"). شهر تورانیان is a genitive construction "the city of the Turanians." Together: "that they did to/against the city of the Turanians."
segments.s244-g67: ز is the poetic preposition "from." ترکان is the source "the Turks." دو بهره = "two shares/portions." بپای is a fused/poetic form of به‌پای / به پای meaning roughly "at the feet of / in the keeping of" (here indicating where or with whom the portions were left). ستور is "steeds/horses." The phrase therefore says they left two shares taken from the Turks with the horses/at the steeds' feet (poetic expression for entrusting custody).
segments.s244-g68: سپردند is the third-person plural past of سپردن ("they entrusted/handed over"). و simply links the clauses "and/then." شد is past of شدن ("became"). بخت را uses را as the definite object/contrast marker ("fortune" as the thing affected). آب شور literally "salty water" is a figurative phrase: the fortune turned to brackish/sour water (i.e. their luck became spoiled).
segments.s244-g69: هنوز (still/yet) is an adverb placed before the phrase for emphasis. آن is the demonstrative "that." سر تیغ is a compound meaning "the tip/head of the sword" (سر = head/top, تیغ = sword). دستان سام is a genitive construct "the hands of Sam" (دستان = "hands") indicating possession: "the tip of the sword in Sam's hands." Poetic style omits connectors (ezāfe) common in classical Persian.
segments.s244-g70: همانا is an emphatic particle "verily/indeed." نیاسود is composed of the negative prefix نی- + آسود (past of آسودن "to rest/be at peace") giving "did not rest / was not at peace." اندر is the preposition "in/within." نیام is the scabbard/sheath. Together: "indeed did not rest in the scabbard."
segments.s244-g71: که introduces the clause "that/so that." رستم is the subject (proper name). همی is the classical progressive/iterative prefix (indicates ongoing or habitual aspect). سرفشاند is a transitive past-form verb literally "to scatter/throw the head" (here used violently: to fling/dispatch, i.e. cast off/strike). ازوی is the old enclitic prepositional pronoun "from him." Grammatically this says Rostam repeatedly performed the action on/against him.
segments.s244-g72: بخورشید here is the prepositional phrase "to the sun" (بـ + خورشید) used figuratively in hyperbolic battle imagery. بر is the preposition "on/upon." خون is "blood." چکاند is past tense of چکاندن "to let drip/ cause to drip." ازوی again is "from him." The clause means literally "he made the sun drink (of it) and caused blood to drip from him," a poetic description of blood spouting/dripping.
segments.s244-g33: سپهبد is the subject preceding the verb. بدانست is the simple past 'he knew/realized' agreeing with سپهبد. کز is the archaic/poetic contraction of که از (here meaning 'that/from'), introducing the reason or content ('from desire'). آرزوی is a noun 'desire/wish' governed by کز.
segments.s244-g34: بپایست here means 'stood (at the feet)' or 'was standing' (a classical poetic form of ایستادن with b- prefix) and is predicated of پیران (the elders). پیران is plural subject; آزاده خوی is a postpositive descriptive phrase ('of noble disposition' / 'noble‑hearted') describing those elders. There is no copula (است) — Persian omits it in such clauses.
segments.s244-g35: بخندید is the past tense 'he laughed'; و simply links clauses. گفتش is 'he said to him' (گفت + enclitic ش = 'to him'). چه is the interrogative 'what'. خواهی is second‑person present/future 'you want/will'; بگوی is an archaic/contracted form of بگویی/بگویی (to say), functioning here in reported interrogative 'what will you say / what do you wish to say'.
segments.s244-g36: ت + را together form ترا (the accusative/oblique 'you') — the text shows them split but they function as the object 'you'. بیشتر is a comparative 'more/greater'. نزد من is a prepositional phrase 'in my sight / with me' (نزد = 'in the presence of / before'); Persian omits the copula, so آبروی (reputation/honor) serves as the predicate: '(you) have greater reputation with me.'
segments.s244-g37: اگر is the conditional 'if'. زر is 'gold'. خواهی is second‑person 'you wish/want' (ellipsis of the pronoun). و‌گر is a poetic 'or if' used to continue alternatives; گوهرا (poetic form of گوهر) means 'gems/jewels' and parallels زر as another object of خواهی.
segments.s244-g38: و‌گر again 'or if'; پادشاهی is a noun 'kingship / kingdom' and takes the following genitive phrase. هر modifies کشورا (poetic form of کشور) and here has the sense 'any/whichever', so پادشاهی هر کشورا = 'the kingship of any country/land'. The clause is an open conditional list (continuing into the next sentence).
segments.s244-g39: ندارم is first‑person present 'I do not have' but here idiomatically 'I will not be unwilling / I will not withhold'. دریغ از تو = 'reluctance toward you' (i.e. 'I have no reluctance concerning you'). من is an emphatic subject pronoun. گنج خویش is 'my treasure / one's own treasure' (خویش here reflexive/possessive), with the whole phrase as the thing not being withheld.
segments.s244-g40: چرا is the question word 'why'. برگزینی is a second‑person present/optative form of برگزیدن 'to choose' (you choose / would you choose) — the ending is classical/poetic. همی is a frequent classical adverb meaning 'ever/indeed' or here softening the verb. رنج خویش (the text splits خوی + ش) means 'one's own suffering' — a reflexive possessive construction; the speaker asks why the addressee would choose to take on their own hardship. There is no copula; the verb برگزینی governs the object رنج خویش.
segments.s244-g97: کزین is an archaic contraction (که از این) meaning "from/because of this" and starts the prepositional phrase. ننگ is the noun "shame/disgrace." تا جاودان is a temporal phrase (تا = "until," جاودان = "eternal") meaning "until eternity/forever." بر سرم is a prepositional phrase (بر = "upon," سرم = "my head") indicating the target of the shame; the whole segment places the cause and its lasting locus before the verb, a typical poetic word order.
segments.s244-g98: بخندد is the classical present/optative 3rd‑person form with the verbal prefix ب- (here: "they/it will laugh" or "may laugh"); همی is a continuous/emphatic particle meaning "always/still." The verb precedes its coordinated subject (کشور و لشکرم) — a common poetic V–S order — and لشکرم carries the pronominal suffix -م "my." Together it says "my realm and my army will/shall (laugh at me)."
segments.s244-g99: چنو (classical چنان/چو) here functions like "if/when" or "should it be that," introducing a conditional. یابد is the classical 3rd‑person present/optative of یابیدن used as "obtains/gets" (here: "obtains release"). از من رهایی literally "freedom/release from me." بجان is an oath/adjunct meaning literally "by the life (of someone)" and intensifies the following action; syntactically it attaches to the clause as an emphatic adverbial.
segments.s244-g100: گشایند is the classical present/optative 3rd‑person plural of گشودن (here "open/loose"). The phrase بر من marks the target "against/on me" (بر used in the sense "against/onto"). ز هر سو is a set phrase "from every side," and زبان (literally "tongues") means "speech/talking," so the clause means "they will set their tongues against me from every side." Note again verb-before-subject/orderly placement typical of the epic style.
segments.s244-g101: برسوایی is a nominal/adverbial phrase meaning "in/under disgrace" (بر + سوایی). اندر is the older preposition "in/within." بمانم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive of ماندن ("I would remain"), and بدرد here uses the verb sense "to be of use/be of benefit" (the phrase implies remaining in disgrace and being of no practical use). The clause expresses the speaker's staying in shame and its (lack of) utility.
segments.s244-g102: بپالایم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive of پالودن/پالاییدن ("I would cleanse/wash away"). از دیدگان is a genitive-like phrase "from the eyes," where دیدگان is the poetic plural "the eyes." آب زرد literally "yellow/pale water" is a conventional poetic image for tears; زرد here describes the quality of the water (pale/wan). The clause says the speaker would cleanse/wipe the pale tears from his eyes.
segments.s244-g103: دگر here means "again/anew." آفرین is an exclamation or noun "praise/acclamation" and کرد is the past 3rd‑person singular of کردن ("they gave/paid" or "they made [an acclamation]"). پیران بدوی is a noun phrase "the ancient/original elders" (پیران = "elders"; بدوی = "primeval/original/old-time"); the whole clause reports that the elders once more voiced praise.
segments.s244-g104: که‌ای is the archaic vocative/exclamatory form (که + ای) used to address someone: here it introduces the direct address. The remaining words are a string of epithets: شاه "king," نیک "good/noble/auspicious," اختر literally "star" used as an honorific, and راست‌گوی "truthful/sincere speaker." Grammatically this is a vocative list of titles/respectful attributes directed at the king.

sentences.s244-s5: Ask the Turks what this post is, and who at the king's court comes from the Dar.
sentences.s244-s6: Garsivaz said to him, 'This is Bijan; he is from Iran — how could he be the king's enemy?'
sentences.s244-s7: He struck his horse and rode up to Bijan; he saw him with a weary heart and his body exposed.
sentences.s244-s8: His two hands were bound behind his back like stone; his mouth was dry and the color had gone from his face.
sentences.s244-s85: They came roaring close to the well; someone thrust a hand into it and made a way.
sentences.s244-s86: When the sun lifted its head above the mountain, Manijeh from every doorway would (continually) — bread ... (text: چدی uncertain).
sentences.s244-s87: You kept circling on that long day; you brought (him) up out of the well's hole to the top.
sentences.s244-s88: You entrusted him to Bijan and you wept; in that misfortune you lived on.
sentences.s244-s77: The blacksmiths drove in heavy nails of steel.
sentences.s244-s78: They cast the ill‑fated one into the well; they shut and made a closure over the well's mouth.
sentences.s244-s79: Then from there, into the porch/portico, he brought that daughter of his — Garsivaz brought that troop (with him).
sentences.s244-s80: He gave all the treasure and jewels as plunder; from this beauty (the moon‑like one) he bound/gave that crown to her.
sentences.s244-s53: Thus is it: the king says (regularly) that he seeks nothing but a good name.
sentences.s244-s54: But to this counsel, my prudent one, let someone look deeply, my commander.
sentences.s244-s55: Bind him to me — bind him tightly! Choose where the gallows should be and put him to death there.
sentences.s244-s56: Whoever you keep bound in your prison, no one will read his name from the records.
sentences.s244-s73: His spring is within, his consoler is within; in this narrow prison his emaciated frame is within.
sentences.s244-s74: Garsivaz strode proudly before him; they carried out the wish of his malicious foe.
sentences.s244-s75: Dragging Bijan, Giv, from before the gallows, they took him, bound, to that well‑field.
sentences.s244-s76: From head to foot they bound him in iron — on his chest, arms, neck, feet and hands.
sentences.s244-s65: He threw him into the grove of Chinestān; bring (him) from Bijan to that land of vengeance.
sentences.s244-s66: Have (the) elephants drag that heaven‑pulling stone which covers the head of Arzhang's well.
sentences.s244-s67: Bring the head of his well and cover it with that, so that the wretched one may regain his senses.
sentences.s244-s68: And from there fetch that unskilled Manijeh, from whom the gem will take shame.
sentences.s244-s13: Go there so that I may see one face of the king; I will show him the auspicious star's path.
sentences.s244-s14: He entered the palace (reading پرستارفش is uncertain); and, toward the king, with his hand he drew/struck.
sentences.s244-s15: He came up to the hem and drew close to the throne; he loudly/strongly praised Afrasiab.
sentences.s244-s16: He stood before his throne at its foot, like an orderly, clean (proper) attendant and guide.
sentences.s244-s37: Quietly, in seeking revenge, you will dazedly smell the poisonous flower.
sentences.s244-s38: If you pour Bijan's blood here, that same dust (storm) of vengeance will arise from Turan.
sentences.s244-s39: You, wise king (or: wise one), while we are the lesser, open the eye of wisdom and look.
sentences.s244-s40: Look, at that vengeance which he spread; what, then, befell (or what did happen to) the king of Iran when he met it?
sentences.s244-s25: I have no need of his gate; there is no dervish among your lower-born.
sentences.s244-s26: Let the shah regard the high-minded one as praised and blameless in every deed.
sentences.s244-s27: For I have, before now, given the shah advice several times about various matters.
sentences.s244-s28: By my command no one rose in renown above him; from him I had a free hand in matters.
sentences.s244-s81: Manijeh, naked and without a veil, with bare feet and her robe open.
sentences.s244-s82: They ran and dragged her to that well‑field; her two eyes were full of blood and her face like a running stream.
sentences.s244-s83: He said to her: “Now, for you, house and home are (as) a pilgrim’s — bound to this place for ever.”
